agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Decompose the courier platform monolith into 5 bounded contexts mapped to 6 deployable services, prioritizing deployment independence between the highest-churn and highest-risk areas (payments, location tracking). The decomposition follows domain boundaries rather than technical layers, uses asynchronous domain events for cross-service communication where possible, and keeps synchronous calls only where transactional consistency is non-negotiable. Given the team size (8 engineers) and moderate operational maturity, this design avoids over-splitting — 6 services is the ceiling, not the floor.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle management: placement, status transitions (placed → assigned → picked-up → in-transit → delivered → cancelled). Owns the order aggregate and orchestrates the fulfillment saga.","boundaries":"Owns orders table. Does NOT own courier state or payment state. Emits OrderPlaced, OrderStatusChanged events. Communicates with Courier Service and Payment Service via events and synchronous calls for critical paths."},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Courier onboarding, availability management, assignment algorithm, and route optimization. Decides WHICH courier gets an order and the optimal route.","boundaries":"Owns courier profiles, availability status, and assignment history. Does NOT own real-time GPS positions (that's Location Service). Consumes location snapshots for assignment decisions. Emits CourierAssigned, CourierUnassigned events."},{"name":"Location Service","responsibility":"Ingests, stores, and serves real-time courier GPS positions. Handles 5,000 writes/sec at peak. Provides current-position queries and short-term position history (last 2 hours).","boundaries":"Owns courier_positions time-series data. Stateless write path optimized for throughput. No business logic beyond geo-validation and staleness detection. Exposes position streams via WebSocket/SSE for consumers."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Payment capture, refund processing, ledger management. Enforces strict transactional consistency — exactly-once payment semantics via idempotency keys. Integrates with external payment gateway (Stripe, Adyen, etc.).","boundaries":"Owns payment records, refund records, and internal ledger. Isolated database with no shared access. Emits PaymentCaptured, PaymentFailed, RefundIssued events. Never exposes raw gateway credentials or tokens outside its boundary."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Delivers customer and courier notifications across channels (email, SMS, push). Reacts to domain events from other services. Owns template management and delivery tracking.","boundaries":"Owns notification templates, delivery logs, and channel preferences. Pure consumer of domain events — no other service depends on it synchronously. Integrates with SendGrid/Twilio/FCM."},{"name":"Customer Service","responsibility":"Customer account management: registration, authentication, profile, address book, preferences. Issues auth tokens consumed by API gateway for all other services.","boundaries":"Owns customer profiles and credentials. Provides customer identity to all other services via JWT tokens validated at the gateway. Does NOT own order history (Order Service does)."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Courier Service","contract":"POST /assignments {orderId, pickupLocation, dropoffLocation} → {courierId, estimatedPickupTime}. Synchronous — order cannot proceed without assignment confirmation.","coupling_type":"synchronous request-response"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"POST /captures {orderId, amount, idempotencyKey} → {paymentId, status}. Synchronous — order completion requires payment confirmation.","coupling_type":"synchronous request-response"},{"from":"Courier Service","to":"Location Service","contract":"GET /positions/{courierId} and GET /positions/nearby?lat=X&lng=Y&radius=R for assignment algorithm. Tolerates stale reads (up to 30s).","coupling_type":"synchronous query (tolerant reader)"},{"from":"Location Service","to":"Order Service","contract":"Publishes CourierPositionUpdated events on a high-throughput topic. Order Service subscribes to update ETA and trigger geo-fence events (e.g., courier near pickup).","coupling_type":"asynchronous event (pub/sub)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Emits OrderPlaced, OrderStatusChanged, CourierAssigned domain events. Notification Service subscribes and maps events to notification templates and channels.","coupling_type":"asynchronous event (pub/sub)"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Emits PaymentCaptured, RefundIssued events. Notification Service sends payment confirmation and refund emails.","coupling_type":"asynchronous event (pub/sub)"},{"from":"Customer Service","to":"All Services","contract":"JWT tokens issued by Customer Service, validated at API Gateway. Services receive customerId as a verified claim — no direct calls to Customer Service at runtime.","coupling_type":"shared token (decoupled identity)"},{"from":"API Gateway","to":"All Services","contract":"Routes external HTTP requests, handles auth token validation, rate limiting, and TLS termination.","coupling_type":"routing (edge proxy)"}],"data_flow":"1. Customer places order → API Gateway → Order Service creates order (PENDING) → emits OrderPlaced. 2. Order Service calls Courier Service synchronously for assignment → Courier Service queries Location Service for nearby couriers → returns assignment → Order Service transitions to ASSIGNED → emits OrderStatusChanged. 3. Notification Service reacts to OrderPlaced and OrderStatusChanged → sends push notification to customer and courier. 4. Courier app sends GPS pings every 10s → API Gateway → Location Service (write-optimized path). Location Service publishes position updates to event stream. 5. Order Service consumes position events to update ETA and detect geo-fence triggers (near pickup, near delivery). 6. On delivery confirmation, Order Service calls Payment Service to capture payment → Payment Service processes with gateway → emits PaymentCaptured → Notification Service sends receipt. 7. For refunds, support triggers refund via Payment Service → emits RefundIssued → Notification Service notifies customer.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Strangler Fig Migration","rationale":"Do not rewrite everything at once. Extract services one at a time from the monolith, starting with Location Service (highest write throughput, most distinct data model) and Payment Service (strictest consistency needs, highest regulatory risk). The monolith continues to serve remaining capabilities during migration."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture (Domain Events via Message Broker)","rationale":"Decouples services that don't need synchronous coordination. Notification Service, ETA updates, and analytics can all react to events without creating runtime dependencies. Enables independent deployability — the primary goal."},{"pattern":"Saga (Orchestration) for Order Fulfillment","rationale":"Order placement spans assignment and payment — two separate services. Order Service orchestrates the saga: assign courier → on delivery → capture payment. Compensating actions handle failures (unassign courier, void payment authorization)."},{"pattern":"CQRS for Location Data","rationale":"Location Service write path is optimized for 5,000 writes/sec (append-only time-series). Read path serves different consumers with different latency/freshness needs. Separating write and read models allows independent scaling."},{"pattern":"API Gateway","rationale":"Single entry point for external clients. Handles cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, TLS) so services don't duplicate this logic. Essential for a team moving from monolith — provides a familiar single-URL experience to clients while routing to services internally."},{"pattern":"Database-per-Service","rationale":"Each service owns its data store. Payment Service gets a relational DB with ACID transactions. Location Service gets a time-series store (TimescaleDB or InfluxDB). This is non-negotiable for deployment independence."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId — contains line items, status, timestamps, assigned courierId, payment reference)","DeliveryEstimate (value object — current ETA, distance, recalculated on position updates)"]},{"name":"Courier Operations","classification":"core","aggregates":["Courier (root: courierId — profile, vehicle type, availability status, current assignment)","Assignment (root: assignmentId — links order to courier, tracks assignment lifecycle)"]},{"name":"Location Tracking","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CourierPosition (root: courierId + timestamp — lat/lng, accuracy, speed, heading). Append-only, no mutation. Not a traditional aggregate — more of an event stream with latest-value projection."]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Payment (root: paymentId — orderId, amount, status, gateway reference, idempotency key)","Refund (root: refundId — paymentId, amount, reason, status)"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: notificationId — channel, recipient, template, status, delivery metadata)"]},{"name":"Customer Identity","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Customer (root: customerId — email, phone, auth credentials, address book, preferences)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Published Language — Order Management emits well-defined domain events; Notifications consumes them. No influence flows upstream."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier Operations","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Order Management requests assignments; Courier Operations fulfills. Courier Operations has autonomy over assignment algorithm but must conform to the assignment request contract."},{"upstream":"Courier Operations","downstream":"Location Tracking","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Courier Operations queries Location Tracking for positions. Location Tracking provides a simple query API with no knowledge of assignment logic."},{"upstream":"Payments","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Conformist — Order Management conforms to Payment Service's capture/refund API. Payment domain model (idempotency keys, gateway abstractions) is not leaked into Order model; Order Service holds only a paymentId reference."},{"upstream":"Customer Identity","downstream":"All Contexts","relationship":"Open Host Service — Customer Identity provides JWT-based identity via a standardized protocol. All contexts consume identity tokens without coupling to customer internals."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"6 services, not more granular (e.g., separate Route Optimization service)","gains":"Manageable operational burden for 8 engineers (~1.3 engineers per service). Fewer network hops. Simpler debugging. Lower infrastructure cost.","sacrifices":"Courier Service bundles assignment logic with routing — if routing becomes ML-heavy, it may need extraction later.","alternatives_considered":["Separate Route Optimization service — rejected: premature split, adds latency to assignment path, team too small","Fewer services (3-4) — rejected: doesn't achieve payment/location deployment independence"]},{"decision":"Synchronous call from Order Service to Payment Service for capture","gains":"Strong consistency guarantee. Order status and payment status are never out of sync. Simpler error handling — fail the order if payment fails.","sacrifices":"Runtime coupling between Order and Payment services. Payment Service downtime blocks order completion.","alternatives_considered":["Async payment capture via events — rejected: too complex for the consistency requirement, risk of orders marked delivered without payment","Two-phase commit — rejected: operationally complex, poor availability characteristics"]},{"decision":"Event-driven communication for non-critical paths (notifications, ETA updates)","gains":"Services can be deployed independently. Notification Service outage doesn't block orders. Natural buffering absorbs spikes.","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency — customers may receive notifications with slight delay. Debugging async flows is harder than synchronous calls.","alternatives_considered":["All synchronous — rejected: creates a distributed monolith, defeats the purpose","All async including payment — rejected: consistency requirements for payments"]},{"decision":"Strangler Fig migration strategy (not big-bang rewrite)","gains":"Reduced risk. Team learns operational skills incrementally. Monolith continues serving traffic during migration. Can stop and reassess after each extraction.","sacrifices":"Longer total migration timeline (6-12 months). Temporary complexity of running monolith + services simultaneously. Need anti-corruption layers during transition.","alternatives_considered":["Big-bang rewrite — rejected: too risky for a team with moderate operational maturity, 3-year monolith has significant implicit knowledge"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Location Service data store","evolution_path":"Start with PostgreSQL + TimescaleDB extension. If write throughput exceeds capacity, swap to dedicated time-series DB (InfluxDB) or stream-first architecture (Kafka + materialized views) without affecting other services."},{"area":"Courier assignment algorithm","evolution_path":"Currently rule-based inside Courier Service. Can evolve to ML-based optimization by deploying a model behind an internal API that Courier Service calls. No other service needs to change."},{"area":"Notification channels","evolution_path":"Adding new channels (WhatsApp, in-app) only requires changes to Notification Service. Domain events carry channel-agnostic data; channel selection is Notification Service's responsibility."},{"area":"Payment gateway","evolution_path":"Payment Service abstracts the gateway behind an internal interface. Switching from Stripe to Adyen (or supporting multiple gateways) is contained within Payment Service."},{"area":"Event broker technology","evolution_path":"Start with a managed broker (Amazon SQS/SNS or Google Pub/Sub). If event volume or ordering requirements grow, migrate to Kafka. Services communicate via topic abstractions, not broker-specific APIs."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order aggregate and its status state machine","reason":"Order status transitions (placed → assigned → picked-up → in-transit → delivered) encode core business invariants. Changing this requires coordinated updates across Order Service, Courier Service, and Notification Service templates. This is intentionally rigid — incorrect status transitions mean incorrect billing and customer experience."},{"area":"Payment idempotency contract","reason":"Idempotency key semantics in Payment Service are safety-critical. Double-charging or missed captures have direct financial impact. This contract should change extremely rarely and only with thorough review."},{"area":"Domain event schemas","reason":"Once published, event schemas become contracts. Breaking changes require versioning strategy (e.g., schema registry, backward-compatible additions only). This is the tax of event-driven architecture — worth it, but must be managed deliberately."},{"area":"Database-per-service boundary","reason":"Each service owns its database exclusively. No shared databases, no cross-service joins. This constraint enables independent deployability but makes cross-service reporting harder (requires event-sourced read models or a data warehouse)."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Team operational maturity gap — moving from 1 deployable to 6 requires monitoring, distributed tracing, log aggregation, and incident response skills the team may not have","severity":"high","mitigation":"Extract services incrementally (Location first, then Payment). Invest in observability stack (OpenTelemetry + Grafana/Datadog) BEFORE extracting the first service. Run game days to practice incident response with distributed systems."},{"risk":"Distributed transaction failures in the order fulfillment saga — courier assigned but payment fails, or payment captured but delivery status not updated","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement saga with explicit compensating actions. Order Service maintains saga state. Use idempotency keys everywhere. Add dead letter queues for failed events with alerting. Test failure scenarios explicitly."},{"risk":"Location Service write throughput — 5,000 writes/sec is achievable but requires careful design. Naive implementation with PostgreSQL may hit connection pool limits or write amplification issues.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use connection pooling (PgBouncer), batch inserts (buffer 1-2 seconds of writes), and TimescaleDB hypertables with appropriate chunk intervals. Load test to 2x peak (10,000 writes/sec) before going live."},{"risk":"Data consistency during Strangler Fig migration — monolith and new services operating on shared state during transition period","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use Change Data Capture (CDC) or dual-write with reconciliation during migration. Define clear cutover criteria for each service extraction. Keep migration window per service under 4 weeks."},{"risk":"Event ordering and duplication — message brokers provide at-least-once delivery, not exactly-once. Duplicate or out-of-order events could cause incorrect state.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Make all event consumers idempotent. Use event sequence numbers for ordering-sensitive consumers (e.g., order status). Implement deduplication at the consumer using event IDs."},{"risk":"Service-to-service latency adding up — order placement now requires network calls to Courier Service and potentially Location Service, adding 50-200ms per hop","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Keep synchronous call chains short (max 2 hops). Use connection pooling and keep-alive. Set aggressive timeouts with circuit breakers (Resilience4j or similar). Cache courier positions in Courier Service with 30s TTL for assignment decisions."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Weeks 1-4): Set up infrastructure — API Gateway, message broker, observability stack, CI/CD per service. Keep all traffic flowing through monolith. Phase 2 (Weeks 5-10): Extract Location Service first — it has the most distinct data model, highest write throughput, and fewest inbound dependencies. Use Strangler Fig: route /api/locations to new service, keep everything else in monolith. Phase 3 (Weeks 11-16): Extract Payment Service — highest consistency requirements, benefits most from isolated deployment. Phase 4 (Weeks 17-22): Extract Notification Service — pure event consumer, low risk. Phase 5 (Weeks 23-30): Extract Courier Service and Order Service from remaining monolith. Customer Service can be extracted last or kept in the monolith if it's low-churn.","files_to_create_or_modify":["services/location-service/ — new service with write-optimized ingestion endpoint and position query API","services/payment-service/ — new service with capture, refund, and ledger endpoints","services/notification-service/ — new service consuming domain events and dispatching to channels","services/courier-service/ — new service with assignment and availability management","services/order-service/ — new service with order lifecycle management and saga orchestration","services/customer-service/ — new service or remains in monolith depending on churn","infrastructure/api-gateway/ — gateway configuration (Kong, AWS API Gateway, or similar)","infrastructure/event-schemas/ — shared event schema definitions with versioning","infrastructure/docker-compose.yml — local development environment with all services","infrastructure/observability/ — OpenTelemetry collector config, Grafana dashboards"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each service follows hexagonal architecture internally: domain core (entities, value objects, domain services) → application layer (use cases, command/query handlers) → infrastructure layer (database adapters, message broker adapters, HTTP controllers). This keeps each service's internals testable and allows swapping infrastructure without touching business logic. Use feature flags during migration to toggle between monolith and service paths.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT create a shared database — this recreates the monolith's coupling at the data layer","Do NOT extract all services simultaneously — the team will be overwhelmed operationally","Do NOT skip the observability investment — you cannot debug distributed systems with printf","Do NOT use synchronous calls for notifications — this creates unnecessary runtime coupling","Do NOT version event schemas by creating new topics — use backward-compatible schema evolution","Do NOT let services call each other's databases directly, even for 'just reads'","Do NOT skip load testing Location Service before cutover — 5,000 writes/sec will expose any design weakness immediately"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Service boundary decomposition aligns cleanly with domain boundaries","Location Service and Payment Service are the right first extractions","Event-driven communication for notifications is clearly the right choice","Database-per-service is non-negotiable for the stated deployment independence goal"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact timeline estimates depend on team velocity and existing code quality — phases could take 50% longer","Whether Customer Service warrants extraction or can remain in a slimmed monolith depends on change frequency data I don't have","Optimal batch size and buffering strategy for Location Service writes needs load testing to validate"],"assumptions":["The monolith uses a single relational database (PostgreSQL or MySQL)","The team has basic containerization experience (Docker) but limited Kubernetes experience","External payment gateway is already integrated in the monolith and the integration can be lifted out","The 500 active couriers and 5,000 writes/sec are current peak — growth rate was not specified so I designed for 2x headroom","Message broker infrastructure (managed service) is available or can be provisioned"]}}

The decomposition follows domain boundaries rather than technical layers, uses asynchronous domain events for cross-service communication where possible, and keeps synchronous calls only where transactional consistency is non-negotiable. Given the team size (8 engineers) and moderate operational maturity, this design avoids over-splitting — 6 services is the ceiling, not the floor.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle management: placement, status transitions (placed → assigned → picked-up → in-transit → delivered → cancelled). Owns the order aggregate and orchestrates the fulfillment saga.","boundaries":"Owns orders table. Does NOT own courier state or payment state. Emits OrderPlaced, OrderStatusChanged events. Communicates with Courier Service and Payment Service via events and synchronous calls for critical paths."},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Courier onboarding, availability management, assignment algorithm, and route optimization. Decides WHICH courier gets an order and the optimal route.","boundaries":"Owns courier profiles, availability status, and assignment history. Does NOT own real-time GPS positions (that's Location Service). Consumes location snapshots for assignment decisions. Emits CourierAssigned, CourierUnassigned events."},{"name":"Location Service","responsibility":"Ingests, stores, and serves real-time courier GPS positions. Handles 5,000 writes/sec at peak. Provides current-position queries and short-term position history (last 2 hours).","boundaries":"Owns courier_positions time-series data. Stateless write path optimized for throughput. No business logic beyond geo-validation and staleness detection. Exposes position streams via WebSocket/SSE for consumers."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Payment capture, refund processing, ledger management. Enforces strict transactional consistency — exactly-once payment semantics via idempotency keys. Integrates with external payment gateway (Stripe, Adyen, etc.).","boundaries":"Owns payment records, refund records, and internal ledger. Isolated database with no shared access. Emits PaymentCaptured, PaymentFailed, RefundIssued events. Never exposes raw gateway credentials or tokens outside its boundary."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Delivers customer and courier notifications across channels (email, SMS, push). Notification Service subscribes and maps events to notification templates and channels.","coupling_type":"asynchronous event (pub/sub)"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Emits PaymentCaptured, RefundIssued events. Notification Service sends payment confirmation and refund emails.","coupling_type":"asynchronous event (pub/sub)"},{"from":"Customer Service","to":"All Services","contract":"JWT tokens issued by Customer Service, validated at API Gateway. Services receive customerId as a verified claim — no direct calls to Customer Service at runtime.","coupling_type":"shared token (decoupled identity)"},{"from":"API Gateway","to":"All Services","contract":"Routes external HTTP requests, handles auth token validation, rate limiting, and TLS termination.","coupling_type":"routing (edge proxy)"}],"data_flow":"1. Customer places order → API Gateway → Order Service creates order (PENDING) → emits OrderPlaced. 2. Order Service calls Courier Service synchronously for assignment → Courier Service queries Location Service for nearby couriers → returns assignment → Order Service transitions to ASSIGNED → emits OrderStatusChanged. 3. If write throughput exceeds capacity, swap to dedicated time-series DB (InfluxDB) or stream-first architecture (Kafka + materialized views) without affecting other services."},{"area":"Courier assignment algorithm","evolution_path":"Currently rule-based inside Courier Service. Can evolve to ML-based optimization by deploying a model behind an internal API that Courier Service calls. No other service needs to change."},{"area":"Notification channels","evolution_path":"Adding new channels (WhatsApp, in-app) only requires changes to Notification Service. Domain events carry channel-agnostic data; channel selection is Notification Service's responsibility."},{"area":"Payment gateway","evolution_path":"Payment Service abstracts the gateway behind an internal interface. Switching from Stripe to Adyen (or supporting multiple gateways) is contained within Payment Service."},{"area":"Event broker technology","evolution_path":"Start with a managed broker (Amazon SQS/SNS or Google Pub/Sub). If event volume or ordering requirements grow, migrate to Kafka. Services communicate via topic abstractions, not broker-specific APIs."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order aggregate and its status state machine","reason":"Order status transitions (placed → assigned → picked-up → in-transit → delivered) encode core business invariants. Changing this requires coordinated updates across Order Service, Courier Service, and Notification Service templates. This is intentionally rigid — incorrect status transitions mean incorrect billing and customer experience."},{"area":"Payment idempotency contract","reason":"Idempotency key semantics in Payment Service are safety-critical. Double-charging or missed captures have direct financial impact. This contract should change extremely rarely and only with thorough review."},{"area":"Domain event schemas","reason":"Once published, event schemas become contracts. Breaking changes require versioning strategy (e.g., schema registry, backward-compatible additions only). This is the tax of event-driven architecture — worth it, but must be managed deliberately."},{"area":"Database-per-service boundary","reason":"Each service owns its database exclusively. No shared databases, no cross-service joins. This constraint enables independent deployability but makes cross-service reporting harder (requires event-sourced read models or a data warehouse)."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Team operational maturity gap — moving from 1 deployable to 6 requires monitoring, distributed tracing, log aggregation, and incident response skills the team may not have","severity":"high","mitigation":"Extract services incrementally (Location first, then Payment). Invest in observability stack (OpenTelemetry + Grafana/Datadog) BEFORE extracting the first service. Run game days to practice incident response with distributed systems."},{"risk":"Distributed transaction failures in the order fulfillment saga — courier assigned but payment fails, or payment captured but delivery status not updated","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement saga with explicit compensating actions. Order Service maintains saga state. Use idempotency keys everywhere. Add dead letter queues for failed events with alerting. Test failure scenarios explicitly."},{"risk":"Location Service write throughput — 5,000 writes/sec is achievable but requires careful design. Naive implementation with PostgreSQL may hit connection pool limits or write amplification issues.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use connection pooling (PgBouncer), batch inserts (buffer 1-2 seconds of writes), and TimescaleDB hypertables with appropriate chunk intervals. Load test to 2x peak (10,000 writes/sec) before going live."},{"risk":"Data consistency during Strangler Fig migration — monolith and new services operating on shared state during transition period","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use Change Data Capture (CDC) or dual-write with reconciliation during migration. Define clear cutover criteria for each service extraction. Keep migration window per service under 4 weeks."},{"risk":"Event ordering and duplication — message brokers provide at-least-once delivery, not exactly-once. Duplicate or out-of-order events could cause incorrect state.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Make all event consumers idempotent. Use event sequence numbers for ordering-sensitive consumers (e.g., order status). Implement deduplication at the consumer using event IDs."},{"risk":"Service-to-service latency adding up — order placement now requires network calls to Courier Service and potentially Location Service, adding 50-200ms per hop","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Keep synchronous call chains short (max 2 hops). Use connection pooling and keep-alive. Set aggressive timeouts with circuit breakers (Resilience4j or similar). Cache courier positions in Courier Service with 30s TTL for assignment decisions."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Weeks 1-4): Set up infrastructure — API Gateway, message broker, observability stack, CI/CD per service. Keep all traffic flowing through monolith. Phase 2 (Weeks 5-10): Extract Location Service first — it has the most distinct data model, highest write throughput, and fewest inbound dependencies. Use Strangler Fig: route /api/locations to new service, keep everything else in monolith. Phase 3 (Weeks 11-16): Extract Payment Service — highest consistency requirements, benefits most from isolated deployment. Phase 4 (Weeks 17-22): Extract Notification Service — pure event consumer, low risk. Phase 5 (Weeks 23-30): Extract Courier Service and Order Service from remaining monolith. Customer Service can be extracted last or kept in the monolith if it's low-churn.","files_to_create_or_modify":["services/location-service/ — new service with write-optimized ingestion endpoint and position query API","services/payment-service/ — new service with capture, refund, and ledger endpoints","services/notification-service/ — new service consuming domain events and dispatching to channels","services/courier-service/ — new service with assignment and availability management","services/order-service/ — new service with order lifecycle management and saga orchestration","services/customer-service/ — new service or remains in monolith depending on churn","infrastructure/api-gateway/ — gateway configuration (Kong, AWS API Gateway, or similar)","infrastructure/event-schemas/ — shared event schema definitions with versioning","infrastructure/docker-compose.yml — local development environment with all services","infrastructure/observability/ — OpenTelemetry collector config, Grafana dashboards"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each service follows hexagonal architecture internally: domain core (entities, value objects, domain services) → application layer (use cases, command/query handlers) → infrastructure layer (database adapters, message broker adapters, HTTP controllers). This keeps each service's internals testable and allows swapping infrastructure without touching business logic. Use feature flags during migration to toggle between monolith and service paths.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT create a shared database — this recreates the monolith's coupling at the data layer","Do NOT extract all services simultaneously — the team will be overwhelmed operationally","Do NOT skip the observability investment — you cannot debug distributed systems with printf","Do NOT use synchronous calls for notifications — this creates unnecessary runtime coupling","Do NOT version event schemas by creating new topics — use backward-compatible schema evolution","Do NOT let services call each other's databases directly, even for 'just reads'","Do NOT skip load testing Location Service before cutover — 5,000 writes/sec will expose any design weakness immediately"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Service boundary decomposition aligns cleanly with domain boundaries","Location Service and Payment Service are the right first extractions","Event-driven communication for notifications is clearly the right choice","Database-per-service is non-negotiable for the stated deployment independence goal"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact timeline estimates depend on team velocity and existing code quality — phases could take 50% longer","Whether Customer Service warrants extraction or can remain in a slimmed monolith depends on change frequency data I don't have","Optimal batch size and buffering strategy for Location Service writes needs load testing to validate"],"assumptions":["The monolith uses a single relational database (PostgreSQL or MySQL)","The team has basic containerization experience (Docker) but limited Kubernetes experience","External payment gateway is already integrated in the monolith and the integration can be lifted out","The 500 active couriers and 5,000 writes/sec are current peak — growth rate was not specified so I designed for 2x headroom","Message broker infrastructure (managed service) is available or can be provisioned"]}}

trace.result: {"executive_summary":"Decompose the courier platform monolith into 5 bounded contexts mapped to 6 deployable services, prioritizing deployment independence between the highest-churn and highest-risk areas (payments, location tracking). The decomposition follows domain boundaries rather than technical layers, uses asynchronous domain events for cross-service communication where possible, and keeps synchronous calls only where transactional consistency is non-negotiable. Given the team size (8 engineers) and moderate operational maturity, this design avoids over-splitting — 6 services is the ceiling, not the floor.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle management: placement, status transitions (placed → assigned → picked-up → in-transit → delivered → cancelled). Owns the order aggregate and orchestrates the fulfillment saga.","boundaries":"Owns orders table. Does NOT own courier state or payment state. Emits OrderPlaced, OrderStatusChanged events. Communicates with Courier Service and Payment Service via events and synchronous calls for critical paths."},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Courier onboarding, availability management, assignment algorithm, and route optimization. Decides WHICH courier gets an order and the optimal route.","boundaries":"Owns courier profiles, availability status, and assignment history. Does NOT own real-time GPS positions (that's Location Service). Consumes location snapshots for assignment decisions. Emits CourierAssigned, CourierUnassigned events."},{"name":"Location Service","responsibility":"Ingests, stores, and serves real-time courier GPS positions. Handles 5,000 writes/sec at peak. Provides current-position queries and short-term position history (last 2 hours).","boundaries":"Owns courier_positions time-series data. Stateless write path optimized for throughput. No business logic beyond geo-validation and staleness detection. Exposes position streams via WebSocket/SSE for consumers."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Payment capture, refund processing, ledger management. Enforces strict transactional consistency — exactly-once payment semantics via idempotency keys. Integrates with external payment gateway (Stripe, Adyen, etc.).","boundaries":"Owns payment records, refund records, and internal ledger. Isolated database with no shared access. Emits PaymentCaptured, PaymentFailed, RefundIssued events. Never exposes raw gateway credentials or tokens outside its boundary."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Delivers customer and courier notifications across channels (email, SMS, push). Reacts to domain events from other services. Owns template management and delivery tracking.","boundaries":"Owns notification templates, delivery logs, and channel preferences. Pure consumer of domain events — no other service depends on it synchronously. Integrates with SendGrid/Twilio/FCM."},{"name":"Customer Service","responsibility":"Customer account management: registration, authentication, profile, address book, preferences. Issues auth tokens consumed by API gateway for all other services.","boundaries":"Owns customer profiles and credentials. Provides customer identity to all other services via JWT tokens validated at the gateway. Does NOT own order history (Order Service does)."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Courier Service","contract":"POST /assignments {orderId, pickupLocation, dropoffLocation} → {courierId, estimatedPickupTime}. Synchronous — order cannot proceed without assignment confirmation.","coupling_type":"synchronous request-response"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"POST /captures {orderId, amount, idempotencyKey} → {paymentId, status}. Synchronous — order completion requires payment confirmation.","coupling_type":"synchronous request-response"},{"from":"Courier Service","to":"Location Service","contract":"GET /positions/{courierId} and GET /positions/nearby?lat=X&lng=Y&radius=R for assignment algorithm. Tolerates stale reads (up to 30s).","coupling_type":"synchronous query (tolerant reader)"},{"from":"Location Service","to":"Order Service","contract":"Publishes CourierPositionUpdated events on a high-throughput topic. Order Service subscribes to update ETA and trigger geo-fence events (e.g., courier near pickup).","coupling_type":"asynchronous event (pub/sub)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Emits OrderPlaced, OrderStatusChanged, CourierAssigned domain events. Notification Service subscribes and maps events to notification templates and channels.","coupling_type":"asynchronous event (pub/sub)"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Emits PaymentCaptured, RefundIssued events. Notification Service sends payment confirmation and refund emails.","coupling_type":"asynchronous event (pub/sub)"},{"from":"Customer Service","to":"All Services","contract":"JWT tokens issued by Customer Service, validated at API Gateway. Services receive customerId as a verified claim — no direct calls to Customer Service at runtime.","coupling_type":"shared token (decoupled identity)"},{"from":"API Gateway","to":"All Services","contract":"Routes external HTTP requests, handles auth token validation, rate limiting, and TLS termination.","coupling_type":"routing (edge proxy)"}],"data_flow":"1. Customer places order → API Gateway → Order Service creates order (PENDING) → emits OrderPlaced. 2. Order Service calls Courier Service synchronously for assignment → Courier Service queries Location Service for nearby couriers → returns assignment → Order Service transitions to ASSIGNED → emits OrderStatusChanged. 3. Notification Service reacts to OrderPlaced and OrderStatusChanged → sends push notification to customer and courier. 4. Courier app sends GPS pings every 10s → API Gateway → Location Service (write-optimized path). Location Service publishes position updates to event stream. 5. Order Service consumes position events to update ETA and detect geo-fence triggers (near pickup, near delivery). 6. On delivery confirmation, Order Service calls Payment Service to capture payment → Payment Service processes with gateway → emits PaymentCaptured → Notification Service sends receipt. 7. For refunds, support triggers refund via Payment Service → emits RefundIssued → Notification Service notifies customer.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Strangler Fig Migration","rationale":"Do not rewrite everything at once. Extract services one at a time from the monolith, starting with Location Service (highest write throughput, most distinct data model) and Payment Service (strictest consistency needs, highest regulatory risk). The monolith continues to serve remaining capabilities during migration."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture (Domain Events via Message Broker)","rationale":"Decouples services that don't need synchronous coordination. Notification Service, ETA updates, and analytics can all react to events without creating runtime dependencies. Enables independent deployability — the primary goal."},{"pattern":"Saga (Orchestration) for Order Fulfillment","rationale":"Order placement spans assignment and payment — two separate services. Order Service orchestrates the saga: assign courier → on delivery → capture payment. Compensating actions handle failures (unassign courier, void payment authorization)."},{"pattern":"CQRS for Location Data","rationale":"Location Service write path is optimized for 5,000 writes/sec (append-only time-series). Read path serves different consumers with different latency/freshness needs. Separating write and read models allows independent scaling."},{"pattern":"API Gateway","rationale":"Single entry point for external clients. Handles cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, TLS) so services don't duplicate this logic. Essential for a team moving from monolith — provides a familiar single-URL experience to clients while routing to services internally."},{"pattern":"Database-per-Service","rationale":"Each service owns its data store. Payment Service gets a relational DB with ACID transactions. Location Service gets a time-series store (TimescaleDB or InfluxDB). This is non-negotiable for deployment independence."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId — contains line items, status, timestamps, assigned courierId, payment reference)","DeliveryEstimate (value object — current ETA, distance, recalculated on position updates)"]},{"name":"Courier Operations","classification":"core","aggregates":["Courier (root: courierId — profile, vehicle type, availability status, current assignment)","Assignment (root: assignmentId — links order to courier, tracks assignment lifecycle)"]},{"name":"Location Tracking","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CourierPosition (root: courierId + timestamp — lat/lng, accuracy, speed, heading). Append-only, no mutation. Not a traditional aggregate — more of an event stream with latest-value projection."]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Payment (root: paymentId — orderId, amount, status, gateway reference, idempotency key)","Refund (root: refundId — paymentId, amount, reason, status)"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: notificationId — channel, recipient, template, status, delivery metadata)"]},{"name":"Customer Identity","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Customer (root: customerId — email, phone, auth credentials, address book, preferences)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Published Language — Order Management emits well-defined domain events; Notifications consumes them. No influence flows upstream."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier Operations","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Order Management requests assignments; Courier Operations fulfills. Courier Operations has autonomy over assignment algorithm but must conform to the assignment request contract."},{"upstream":"Courier Operations","downstream":"Location Tracking","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Courier Operations queries Location Tracking for positions. Location Tracking provides a simple query API with no knowledge of assignment logic."},{"upstream":"Payments","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Conformist — Order Management conforms to Payment Service's capture/refund API. Payment domain model (idempotency keys, gateway abstractions) is not leaked into Order model; Order Service holds only a paymentId reference."},{"upstream":"Customer Identity","downstream":"All Contexts","relationship":"Open Host Service — Customer Identity provides JWT-based identity via a standardized protocol. All contexts consume identity tokens without coupling to customer internals."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"6 services, not more granular (e.g., separate Route Optimization service)","gains":"Manageable operational burden for 8 engineers (~1.3 engineers per service). Fewer network hops. Simpler debugging. Lower infrastructure cost.","sacrifices":"Courier Service bundles assignment logic with routing — if routing becomes ML-heavy, it may need extraction later.","alternatives_considered":["Separate Route Optimization service — rejected: premature split, adds latency to assignment path, team too small","Fewer services (3-4) — rejected: doesn't achieve payment/location deployment independence"]},{"decision":"Synchronous call from Order Service to Payment Service for capture","gains":"Strong consistency guarantee. Order status and payment status are never out of sync. Simpler error handling — fail the order if payment fails.","sacrifices":"Runtime coupling between Order and Payment services. Payment Service downtime blocks order completion.","alternatives_considered":["Async payment capture via events — rejected: too complex for the consistency requirement, risk of orders marked delivered without payment","Two-phase commit — rejected: operationally complex, poor availability characteristics"]},{"decision":"Event-driven communication for non-critical paths (notifications, ETA updates)","gains":"Services can be deployed independently. Notification Service outage doesn't block orders. Natural buffering absorbs spikes.","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency — customers may receive notifications with slight delay. Debugging async flows is harder than synchronous calls.","alternatives_considered":["All synchronous — rejected: creates a distributed monolith, defeats the purpose","All async including payment — rejected: consistency requirements for payments"]},{"decision":"Strangler Fig migration strategy (not big-bang rewrite)","gains":"Reduced risk. Team learns operational skills incrementally. Monolith continues serving traffic during migration. Can stop and reassess after each extraction.","sacrifices":"Longer total migration timeline (6-12 months). Temporary complexity of running monolith + services simultaneously. Need anti-corruption layers during transition.","alternatives_considered":["Big-bang rewrite — rejected: too risky for a team with moderate operational maturity, 3-year monolith has significant implicit knowledge"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Location Service data store","evolution_path":"Start with PostgreSQL + TimescaleDB extension. If write throughput exceeds capacity, swap to dedicated time-series DB (InfluxDB) or stream-first architecture (Kafka + materialized views) without affecting other services."},{"area":"Courier assignment algorithm","evolution_path":"Currently rule-based inside Courier Service. Can evolve to ML-based optimization by deploying a model behind an internal API that Courier Service calls. No other service needs to change."},{"area":"Notification channels","evolution_path":"Adding new channels (WhatsApp, in-app) only requires changes to Notification Service. Domain events carry channel-agnostic data; channel selection is Notification Service's responsibility."},{"area":"Payment gateway","evolution_path":"Payment Service abstracts the gateway behind an internal interface. Switching from Stripe to Adyen (or supporting multiple gateways) is contained within Payment Service."},{"area":"Event broker technology","evolution_path":"Start with a managed broker (Amazon SQS/SNS or Google Pub/Sub). If event volume or ordering requirements grow, migrate to Kafka. Services communicate via topic abstractions, not broker-specific APIs."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order aggregate and its status state machine","reason":"Order status transitions (placed → assigned → picked-up → in-transit → delivered) encode core business invariants. Changing this requires coordinated updates across Order Service, Courier Service, and Notification Service templates. This is intentionally rigid — incorrect status transitions mean incorrect billing and customer experience."},{"area":"Payment idempotency contract","reason":"Idempotency key semantics in Payment Service are safety-critical. Double-charging or missed captures have direct financial impact. This contract should change extremely rarely and only with thorough review."},{"area":"Domain event schemas","reason":"Once published, event schemas become contracts. Breaking changes require versioning strategy (e.g., schema registry, backward-compatible additions only). This is the tax of event-driven architecture — worth it, but must be managed deliberately."},{"area":"Database-per-service boundary","reason":"Each service owns its database exclusively. No shared databases, no cross-service joins. This constraint enables independent deployability but makes cross-service reporting harder (requires event-sourced read models or a data warehouse)."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Team operational maturity gap — moving from 1 deployable to 6 requires monitoring, distributed tracing, log aggregation, and incident response skills the team may not have","severity":"high","mitigation":"Extract services incrementally (Location first, then Payment). Invest in observability stack (OpenTelemetry + Grafana/Datadog) BEFORE extracting the first service. Run game days to practice incident response with distributed systems."},{"risk":"Distributed transaction failures in the order fulfillment saga — courier assigned but payment fails, or payment captured but delivery status not updated","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement saga with explicit compensating actions. Order Service maintains saga state. Use idempotency keys everywhere. Add dead letter queues for failed events with alerting. Test failure scenarios explicitly."},{"risk":"Location Service write throughput — 5,000 writes/sec is achievable but requires careful design. Naive implementation with PostgreSQL may hit connection pool limits or write amplification issues.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use connection pooling (PgBouncer), batch inserts (buffer 1-2 seconds of writes), and TimescaleDB hypertables with appropriate chunk intervals. Load test to 2x peak (10,000 writes/sec) before going live."},{"risk":"Data consistency during Strangler Fig migration — monolith and new services operating on shared state during transition period","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use Change Data Capture (CDC) or dual-write with reconciliation during migration. Define clear cutover criteria for each service extraction. Keep migration window per service under 4 weeks."},{"risk":"Event ordering and duplication — message brokers provide at-least-once delivery, not exactly-once. Duplicate or out-of-order events could cause incorrect state.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Make all event consumers idempotent. Use event sequence numbers for ordering-sensitive consumers (e.g., order status). Implement deduplication at the consumer using event IDs."},{"risk":"Service-to-service latency adding up — order placement now requires network calls to Courier Service and potentially Location Service, adding 50-200ms per hop","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Keep synchronous call chains short (max 2 hops). Use connection pooling and keep-alive. Set aggressive timeouts with circuit breakers (Resilience4j or similar). Cache courier positions in Courier Service with 30s TTL for assignment decisions."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Weeks 1-4): Set up infrastructure — API Gateway, message broker, observability stack, CI/CD per service. Keep all traffic flowing through monolith. Phase 2 (Weeks 5-10): Extract Location Service first — it has the most distinct data model, highest write throughput, and fewest inbound dependencies. Use Strangler Fig: route /api/locations to new service, keep everything else in monolith. Phase 3 (Weeks 11-16): Extract Payment Service — highest consistency requirements, benefits most from isolated deployment. Phase 4 (Weeks 17-22): Extract Notification Service — pure event consumer, low risk. Phase 5 (Weeks 23-30): Extract Courier Service and Order Service from remaining monolith. Customer Service can be extracted last or kept in the monolith if it's low-churn.","files_to_create_or_modify":["services/location-service/ — new service with write-optimized ingestion endpoint and position query API","services/payment-service/ — new service with capture, refund, and ledger endpoints","services/notification-service/ — new service consuming domain events and dispatching to channels","services/courier-service/ — new service with assignment and availability management","services/order-service/ — new service with order lifecycle management and saga orchestration","services/customer-service/ — new service or remains in monolith depending on churn","infrastructure/api-gateway/ — gateway configuration (Kong, AWS API Gateway, or similar)","infrastructure/event-schemas/ — shared event schema definitions with versioning","infrastructure/docker-compose.yml — local development environment with all services","infrastructure/observability/ — OpenTelemetry collector config, Grafana dashboards"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each service follows hexagonal architecture internally: domain core (entities, value objects, domain services) → application layer (use cases, command/query handlers) → infrastructure layer (database adapters, message broker adapters, HTTP controllers). This keeps each service's internals testable and allows swapping infrastructure without touching business logic.
